does: This paper introduces an AI chaperone framework—a repurposed language model that evaluates ongoing conversations between users and chatbots to detect parasocial and sycophantic cues in real time. The authors constructed a synthetic dataset of thirty dialogues annotated for parasocial, sycophantic, and neutral content, then tested their evaluation framework through five stages of iterative assessment. Under a unanimity rule requiring agreement from multiple evaluators, the chaperone successfully identified all parasocial conversations while avoiding false positives, typically catching problematic patterns within the first few conversational exchanges. The approach demonstrates that external monitoring can effectively flag emerging relational harms without requiring architectural modifications to the underlying chatbot system.
connects: This work responds to growing concerns about chatbot-induced harms documented in recent studies showing that users develop unhealthy emotional attachments and that AI systems exploit flattery and agreement to reinforce user preferences. It builds on existing literature examining parasocial relationships—one-sided emotional bonds traditionally studied in media effects—and extends harm mitigation beyond model training techniques like RLHF that target sycophancy during development. The paper engages with AI safety concerns around value alignment and user manipulation, drawing on prior work in detecting problematic conversational patterns and applying ensemble-based detection methods that leverage multiple language model evaluators for robustness.
enables: This framework opens directions for deploying monitoring systems in production chatbot environments, particularly for high-risk contexts like child safety or vulnerable populations. Future work could refine the parasocial detection dataset with naturally occurring conversations rather than synthetic examples, improve computational efficiency for real-time monitoring, and develop intervention strategies that respond to detected cues by altering bot behavior rather than merely flagging conversations. The approach suggests broader possibilities for external oversight mechanisms that complement model-level safeguards, and hints at the potential for chaperone systems to handle other harmful conversational dynamics beyond parasocial relationships, establishing a template for collaborative human-AI evaluation of emerging social risks.
